summary: Cantrill recounts attending Surge and GOTO conferences, presenting on production debugging challenges with latency-sensitive applications. The highlight was visiting Aarhus to meet the V8/Dart team (Lars Bak, Kasper Lund, et al.) to discuss postmortem debuggability of dynamic languages. He discovers that this concentration of VM talent traces back to Professor Ole Lehrmann Madsen and the BETA language project, drawing parallels to his own 'CS169 School' from Brown University that produced generations of OS engineers.
key_points: Presented on DIRT (data-intensive real-time) production issues at Surge with Brendan Gregg
Met with V8/Dart team in Aarhus to discuss achieving 'total postmortem debuggability' in dynamic environments
Discovered the 'BETA School' - VM engineers who trace their origins to Professor Ole Lehrmann Madsen's BETA language work
Drew parallels between BETA School and CS169 School as university-rooted clusters of systems engineers
money_quotes: It is rare to be in the presence of a team that has single-handedly changed the trajectory of software – and it is rarer still to be able bring one's own technology to such an august table.

As the birthplace of V8, Aarhus has always had a Dagobah-like appeal to me: I feel that when on the freezing ice planet that is mdb_v8.c, Dave and I might as well have seen Obi-Wan Kenobi as an apparition, telling us we must go to Aarhus if we wanted to truly understand objects-inl.h.

This is a problem that we've been after in one way or another for nearly a decade, but this was the first time that we were having the conversation early enough in the VM implementation phase that one can hope to get ahead of the problem instead of chasing it.
I am an inveterate disaster porn addict.
There seems to be no limit of hard, interesting problems in our domain – and many profoundly talented engineers to tackle them!
key_insight: The most influential clusters of systems software engineers often trace their origins to a single professor or university program, creating schools of thought that shape entire technology trajectories.
